coredata.dc:title: Arsenic field test kits based on solid-phase fluorescence filter effect induced by silver nanoparticle formation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Wenhui
Ye, Liqing
Wu, Yuke
Wang, Xi
Ye, Simin
Deng, Yurong
Huang, Ke
Luo, Hong
Zhang, Jinyi
Zheng, Chengbin
coredata.dc:description: Millions of people worldwide are affected by naturally occurring arsenic in groundwater. The development of a low-cost, highly sensitive, portable assay for rapid field detection of arsenic in water is important to identify areas for safe wells and to help prioritize testing. Herein, a novel paper-based fluorescence assay was developed for the on-site analysis of arsenic, which was constructed by the solid-phase fluorescence filter effect (SPFFE) of AsH3-induced the generation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) toward carbon dots. The proposed SPFFE-based assay achieves a low arsenic detection limit of 0.36 μg/L due to the efficient reduction of Ag+ by AsH3 and the high molar extinction coefficient of AgNPs. In conjunction with a smartphone and an integrated sample processing and sensing platform, field-sensitive detection of arsenic could be achieved. The accuracy of the portable assay was validated by successfully analyzing surface and groundwater samples, with no significant difference from the results obtained through mass spectrometry. Compared to other methods for arsenic analysis, this developed system offers excellent sensitivity, portability, and low cost. It holds promising potential for on-site analysis of arsenic in groundwater to identify safe well locations and quickly obtain output from the global map of groundwater arsenic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arsenic
Solid-phase fluorescence filter effect
On-site
Silver nanoparticle
Portable device

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the CDs/AgNO3 test paper
Construction of the portable detection device
AsH3-induced SPFFE from the generation of AgNPs
Analytical performance of the developed method
3D printed portable device integrated reaction and detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22376144, 22104101, and 22076128) and Science & Technology Department of Sichuan Province (No. 24NSFSC6600) for their financial support. We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for microscopy work and we would be grateful to Hui Wang for her help with SEM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Pictures and UV absorption spectra of Ag+ papers in the presence and absence of AsH3 (20 μg/L); (B) SEM images of CDs/Ag+ papers before and after reaction with AsH3 (20 μg/L); (C) fluorescence spectra (λex = 365 nm) of CD with addition of AsH3 (5 µg/L) and pictures of the test papers in daylight and UV light (λex = 365 nm) and (D) fluorescence decay of CDs and CDs/Ag+ before and after reaction with AsH3 (5 μg/L).

(A) Effect of paper different sizes on fluorescence signal values and pictures (10 μg/L As) and effect of parameters on the fluorescence signal of arsenic determination (B) AgNO3 concentration; (C) KBH4 concentration; (D) HCl concentration; (E) reaction temperature; (F) reaction time; Reaction solution containing 0.5% (m/v) NaOH and 20 μg/L As.

(A) The selectivity of the sensor towards single ions; (B) ion coexistence together with arsenic using this sensor to analyze 10 μg/L arsenic. 1 mg/L of Zn(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), Se(IV), Cu(II), and Fe(III), 0.1 mg/L of Ni(II), Sn(VI); 20 μg/L of Sb(III) and Bi(III); 10 μg/L of Hg(II), 1 mg/L of other salts. (C) fluorescence photograph of this SPFFE-based sensor upon the addition of various concentrations of arsenic under optimal reaction conditions and its linear relationship of F0-F versus the concentration of arsenic in the range of 1–20 μg/L. (D) the relative standard deviation of 11 standard arsenic solution samples by this sensor.

(A) The physical photograph and size of the whole device with different perspectives. (B) the overall design of the fabricated fluorescence readout device integrated heating and sensing (1* is the UV lamp switch, 2* is the heating switch, and 3* is the main power switch).
The overall process of pre-treatment and detection using this proposed portable device in the field. Spatial distribution diagram of sampling sites (Funan River, Chengdu, China).
Schematic diagram of the proposed AsH3-induced SPFFE for the visualization of arsenic, portable device, and sensing mechanism.
Comparison of the results of the proposed SPFFE sensor and ICP-MS for real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a mean and standard deviation (n = 3). ND, not detected. *diluted 250 times; * *diluted two times; b t 0.05, 4 = 2.78.

head.ce:title: Arsenic field test kits based on solid-phase fluorescence filter effect induced by silver nanoparticle formation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610068, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of MOE, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of MOE, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of MOE, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Millions of people worldwide are affected by naturally occurring arsenic in groundwater. The development of a low-cost, highly sensitive, portable assay for rapid field detection of arsenic in water is important to identify areas for safe wells and to help prioritize testing. Herein, a novel paper-based fluorescence assay was developed for the on-site analysis of arsenic, which was constructed by the solid-phase fluorescence filter effect (SPFFE) of AsH3-induced the generation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) toward carbon dots. The proposed SPFFE-based assay achieves a low arsenic detection limit of 0.36 μg/L due to the efficient reduction of Ag+ by AsH3 and the high molar extinction coefficient of AgNPs. In conjunction with a smartphone and an integrated sample processing and sensing platform, field-sensitive detection of arsenic could be achieved. The accuracy of the portable assay was validated by successfully analyzing surface and groundwater samples, with no significant difference from the results obtained through mass spectrometry. Compared to other methods for arsenic analysis, this developed system offers excellent sensitivity, portability, and low cost. It holds promising potential for on-site analysis of arsenic in groundwater to identify safe well locations and quickly obtain output from the global map of groundwater arsenic.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Arsenic, classified as a Group I carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer, causes skin cancer, cardiovascular disease, neurotoxicity, and lung disease [1–6]. According to a comprehensive analysis of global data, 107 countries are affected by arsenic contamination of groundwater, posing a significant health risk to people, and many countries have set regulations and standards for the maximum allowable concentration of arsenic in drinking water (10 μg/L) [4,7–9]. Although many research groups have predicted safe and unsafe zones for groundwater arsenic contamination in China and globally based on statistical risk models [2,8,10], these models need to be confirmed by additional field measurements. In addition, water scarcity due to population growth and climate change necessitates the development of a low-cost, highly sensitive, and portable detection method to rapidly determine the spatial distribution and extent of arsenic contamination in groundwater.

Atomic spectrometry and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) are the gold standard for analyzing arsenic in water, but large, expensive equipment and specialized operating techniques limit their use in field analysis [11–13]. Recently, miniaturized atomic spectrometers have also been widely used for on-site environmental analysis, but they were more cumbersome to use and lacked sensitive detection compared to commercially available colorimetric paper for the measurement of arsenic [14–19]. With the development of fluorescent material technology, various fluorescent sensors have been widely used for arsenic determination [16,20,21]. However, many of these sensors have limitations such as susceptibility to interference from other metal ions or anions and detection limits that do not meet World Health Organization (WHO) regulations [22–27]. The inner filter effect (IFE) is a non-radiative energy conversion pathway resulting from the absorption of excitation and/or emission light by an absorber, has the advantages of simplicity and flexibility in the implementation of radiometric fluorescence sensing without modification of any fluorophore [28–30]. Many existing IFE-based methods operate primarily in the aqueous phase, and the complex composition present in the actual environmental water matrix (e.g., turbidity and color) can lead to the potential for false positive results, thereby hindering their accurate determination in water [16,26]. Although the integration of fluorescence sensors with portable smart devices has become a mainstream trend for various target analyses with the advantages of simplicity and portability, accurate target determination with high sensitivity and elimination of matrix interference remains a major challenge in this field of analysis [31–33].

Recently, a solid-phase fluorescence filter effect (SPFFE) has been proposed and defined as a phenomenon based on non-radiative energy transfer between solid-phase absorbers and solid-phase fluorophores, which can be further utilized to design a fluorescence paper for the determination of iodine and its speciation [34,35]. In addition, the liquid targets could be transited into their volatile state to be further involved in the IFE system, which can effectively eliminate the interference from the liquid matrix caused by traditional liquid reaction systems and allow enrichment targets. AsH3 triggers the generation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) with high molar extinction coefficients when combined with Ag+, ensuring the development of highly sensitive IFE systems. Herein, arsenic in solution is converted to AsH3 and then enriched by headspace adsorption, which reacts with Ag+ to form AgNPs that specifically quench the blue fluorescence of the carbon dots (CDs) based on IFE. The chemisorption-triggered SPFFE not only exhibits good selectivity and excellent interference immunity, but the fluorescent test kits also have a high sensitivity for the detection of arsenic (detection limit of 0.36 μg/L). In addition, a portable 3D-printed device combined with a signal-reading program allows for rapid on-site analysis of arsenic in water ( Scheme 1). Sample testing and field analysis results show that this strategy has a promising application for real-time monitoring of trace arsenic in the surface and groundwater. With approximately 200 million people potentially exposed to high levels of arsenic in groundwater, the vast majority (94%) of whom are in Asia, this work is important to raise awareness, identify areas for safe wells, and help prioritize testing.

The experimental reagents were above the analytical grade reagents. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium borohydride (KBH4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), silver nitrate (AgNO3), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), barium chloride (BaCl2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), lead acetate trihydrate (Pb(CH3COO)2∙ 3 H2O), ethylenediamine and citric acid were purchased from Kelong (Chengdu, China). The standard solution of the heavy metal elements (Zn(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), Se(IV), Cu(II), Fe(III), Ni(II), Sb(III), Bi(III)) were purchased from the National Research Center for Certified Reference Materials (Beijing, China). All solutions were prepared using 18.2 MΩ cm deionized water (DIW), obtained from a water purification system (Chengdu Ultrapure Technology Co., Ltd., Chengdu, China).

The skim cotton was purchased from Poetic Silk (Hangzhou, China). Whatman 3 filter paper supplied by Whatman LTD Company was used as a test paper. The mixture solution of the reaction was packed in commercial ink cartridges (Hp 803 Black). The pattern of the test paper was created on the paper by Microsoft Word and then printed on the printer (HP LaserJet 1112 series). The test paper was punched by Paper Puncher (Liqiu Culture Company, China). Polytetrafluoroethylene gaskets (Hong Spectrum Experimental Technology, China) are used to hold test paper sheets in place. The heat pads (Yilong Electric Appliance Company, China) were used to heat the reaction solution. A digital display thermostat (JY-260S) was used to control the temperature. A permanent magnet and electric motor (Xinyue Experiment Company, China) formed a magnetic stirrer. A PWM DC motor governor (Nuoxin Electronic, China) was used to adjust the speed of the magnetic stirrer. 10 mm magneton (Aosheng Experiment Company, China) was used to stir the reaction solution. The charging treasure (Wireless Energy Power Company, China) was used as a mobile power supply.

Lambda 365 ultraviolet/visible spectrophotometer (UV-Vis) (Perkin Elmer, USA) was used to collect UV absorption spectrograms of test strips. Fluorescence spectra of C-dots were measured using a FluoroMax+ spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). The particle size of CDs was observed on a JEM-2100 plus transmission electron microscope (TEM, Nihon Electronics). The characteristics of the generated AgNPs were observed on a Tecnai G2F20 S-TWIN scanning electron microscope (FEI, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. The fluorescence lifetimes of As-added CDs were measured by Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer, with DeltaDiode (Ex: 360 nm) as the excitation light source. The CDs/Ag+ sprayed paper was obtained with an inkjet printer (HP, DeskJet 1112). Photographs were taken using a UV lamp bead (LG Photonics, Seoul, Korea) with an emission wavelength of 365 nm as the light source. The portable color reading device was prepared by a fully enclosed CR-3040 Pro 3D printer (Jialichuang 3D, China). All photos of the post-reaction test strips were taken by a smartphone (Redmi K30) camera. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Agilent, USA) was used for the quantitative analysis of As.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CDs/AgNO3 test paper
ce:section.ce:para: A commercial ink cartridge was washed of toner particles in ultrapure water using an ultrasonic cleaner and left to dry. A mixture of 50 μL of CDs (25 mg/mL) and 25 μL (500 mM) of Ag+ by adding 425 μL of pure water was then injected into the empty cartridge as 'ink'. A CDs/Ag+ printed paper was then obtained and cut into a round shape with a diameter of 16.0 mm to fit the bottle cap. The prepared CDs/Ag+ paper is stored in the refrigerator at −20ºC before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the portable detection device
ce:section.ce:para: A plastic device was designed to be printed on a UV-curable 3D printer using photosensitive resin. A 12 V (6000 mAh) Li-ion battery pack (68.0 mm * 55.0 mm * 35.0 mm) was used to power the thermal unit (operating at 30ºC, maximum power: 3 W for about 12 h, including a thermistor), permanent magnet motor (12 V, 0.30 A, 5 W, including a DC motor controller), and UV light (365 nm, 4.5 V, 600 mA). A bedplate with a groove was designed for loading the test paper, which was positioned perpendicular to the smartphone to capture fluorescence images.

A series concentration of arsenic standard solution together with 0.36 M hydrochloric acid was added to the headspace bottle, which was sealed with a cap loaded with prepared CDs/Ag+ paper. Next to the cap, a polytetrafluoroethylene gasket with a pore size of 8.0 mm was stacked with the paper to reduce the contact area between AsH3 and the paper. Skimmed cotton soaked in lead acetate trihydrate was placed on a glass sieve to eliminate interference from H2S and to isolate water vapor. After sealing the bottle, 2.50 mL of 1% (m/v) KBH4 containing 0.50% (m/v) NaOH solution was injected into the bottle to produce AsH3, which reacted with CDs/Ag+ paper at a controlled temperature (30ºC). The reaction solution containing different concentrations of arsenic was stirred for 30 min, and then the test paper was taken out to be recorded using the developed portable device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AsH3-induced SPFFE from the generation of AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the modified Gutzeit method, arsenic could be measured by the formation of black silver precipitate in solution by the reaction between AsH3 and Ag+, whereas AsH3-triggered the in-situ synthesis of AgNPs on the paper has not been reported [36–38]. We first investigated the feasibility of forming AgNPs by reacting the generated AsH3 with Ag+ printed on the paper. As shown in Fig. 1A, the printed paper color changed from white to light grey and a clear absorption at 425 nm corresponding to AgNPs can be obtained in the presence of AsH3. The morphology of the generated AgNPs on the paper was characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Fig. 1B), suggesting that the generated AsH3 mixed with Ag+-printed paper indeed facilitates the formation of AgNPs even in the solid phase. Furthermore, the relationship between the obtained morphology of AgNPs and the concentration of arsenic was further investigated. It can be observed from Fig. S1 that the formation of AgNPs was accumulative with the increasing concentration of arsenic (5 and 10 μg/L) and the size of the formed AgNPs remained around 35 nm, indicating that the concentration of AsH3 was proportional to the mass of the formed AgNPs and had little effect on the particle size. It can be also observed that the nanoparticles were uniformly distributed on the surface of the paper with an average diameter of about 21 nm with a high concentration of AsH3 (Fig. 1B, 20 μg/L). This observation suggests that the process can be attributed to the fact that Ag+ can be reduced to silver nuclei by reacting with a certain concentration of arsenic. The higher the concentration of arsenic used, the more silver nuclei were produced due to the presence of a reducing agent. This limits the size of the particles produced, which aligns with findings from previous reports [38–40]. The AgNPs formed by different concentrations of arsenic and silver ions were similar in shape, resulting in similar absorbance and consistent sensitivity of the analyses. Instead of the aqueous phase, AsH3 as a gaseous reducing agent could induce the formation of AgNPs on the paper properties with faster reaction kinetics and eliminate the matrix interference to a certain extent [14,17,41]. After verifying the volatile AsH3-induced instantaneous formation of AgNPs, it's found that AgNPs are an excellent absorber for the construction of IFE sensors with high sensitivity due to their high molar absorption coefficient [14,42]. Compared to traditional fluorescent molecules and nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as ideal candidates for various IFE-based sensors due to their numerous advantages, including low cost, simple synthesis routes, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, high photo- and chemical stability, independent fluorescence, and tunable excitation and emission [43–45]. Here we have synthesized non-toxic CDs with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 nm and 445 nm, which perfectly match the absorption spectrum of AgNPs and can be constructed as IFE-based probes (Fig. S2A) [46–48]. The emission wavelength remained stable at 440 nm regardless of the variation in the excitation light, and there was a significant overlap with the absorption spectrum of the AgNPs. Commercial UV lamps typically emit light with a wavelength of 365 nm. Therefore, the excitation wavelength was selected to be 365 nm, resulting in no significant decrease in fluorescence intensity. Besides, the excitation spectra of CDs were unchanged before and after the reaction with AsH3 (Fig. S2B). Detailed preparation and characterization of CDs are shown in the Supporting Information and Figs. S3. The fluorescence of CDs is affected by the pH of the solution [49], but the system utilizes hydride generation, operating under acidic conditions to ensure that the test results are not interfered with acids and bases. In addition, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the resulting CDs and the arsenic concentration was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 1C, no change in fluorescence or color of paper containing only CDs before or after reacting with AsH3. The fluorescence emission of CDs was significantly reduced only when CDs/Ag+ reacted with AsH3, indicating that the in situ formed AgNPs quenched the fluorescence of CDs due to the IFE between AgNPs and CDs. To further explore the mechanism of AsH3-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs with and without the addition of arsenic were investigated. As shown in Fig. 1D, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs decreased from 6.35 ns to 4.89 ns with increasing arsenic concentration, suggesting that the AsH3-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs may involve fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Compared with the fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after reacting with 5 μg/L As, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased by 51%, while the fluorescence lifetime only decayed by 23%. Therefore, although the quenching of CDs is caused by a combination of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer and IFE effects, the dominant factor is still the IFE.

It has been shown theoretically that reducing the diameter of the headspace region significantly increases the particle density of the fluorescent receptor, thereby improving the SPFFE [34]. The results are shown in Fig. 2A, where the quenching efficiency decreases with increasing headspace area (within the test area) at a concentration of 10 μg/L As. As the signal was not easily extracted from a small area of the test paper, its generation and detection are subject to a degree of error. Therefore, a circle of 8 mm diameter was chosen as the reaction zone. To obtain the best signal, the concentration of Ag+ was optimized as shown in Fig. 2B, and 25 mM Ag+ was chosen for the experiment by considering the wider linear range. The conversion of arsenic to AsH3 is a critical step for the release of the arsenic signal from the water, so the effect of KBH4 and HCl concentration on the generated AsH3 efficiency was first investigated [41,50,51]. As shown in Fig. 2C and 2D, the signal obtained was significantly increased in the concentration range of 0.005–0.01% KBH4, 0.10–0.25 M HCl and then reached a plateau or decreased with further increase in concentration. As the AsH3-induced formation of AgNPs on the CDs printed paper was attributed to the conversion of arsenic to AsH3 in the presence of KBH4, the reaction temperature and time were investigated and the results are presented in Figs. 2E and F. The temperature is tightly coupled to the generation efficiency of volatile AsH3, the optimal signal can be obtained at 30 °C, and the acquired signal increases dramatically with time, reaching a plateau after 30 min. The acidity of the aqueous solution plays a crucial role in the selective production of hydrides by arsenic species. In a strongly acidic environment, both inorganic arsenic species are reduced to hydrogen arsenide [41]. The effect of hydrochloric acid on As(III) and As(V) signaling was evaluated and the analytical responses were found to be similar (Fig. S4). The entire test takes only 30 min, and there is no need for thorough mixing before the reaction; both the aqueous phase and the reaction on the paper are performed simultaneously. Considering that humidity can cause sharp changes in temperature, we simulated its effect for the study and found it to be non-disruptive under the conditions to which this method was applied (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the common heavy metals, hydride-forming elements, and salts present in real water samples, the selectivity and ion coexistence experiments were carried out under optimized conditions. As the total arsenic concentration increased, the blue fluorescence gradually diminished, and the difference in fluorescence intensity (F0-F) before (F0) and after (F) the reaction was selected as the output value. As shown in Fig. 3A, no significant signals were observed for the numerous heavy metals and salts tested at a concentration of 1 mg/L. Additionally, the ability to release metals and arsenic via hydride generation did not show a significant response at concentrations 1–10 times higher than the arsenic concentration. Meanwhile, the strongly reducing AsH3-induced generation of AgNPs ensures the high selectivity of this sensing strategy. Fig. 3B shows that all the ions tested together with arsenic had almost no effect on the fluorescence signal obtained, even at their 100-fold concentration of arsenic, demonstrating that this method has excellent selectivity to discriminate arsenic from common ions in real water samples. To investigate whether the colored samples would interfere with the fluorescence of the experimental results, two dyes, methyl violet and resin azure, were selected and added to the reaction system (Fig. S6). It can be observed that the spiked recoveries of the two dyes were in the range of 88.0–116.0% with satisfactory recoveries (Table S1). The analytical performance of the method was tested by gradually increasing the concentration of As (from 0–20 μg/L) under optimal conditions. As exhibited in Fig. 3C, the blue fluorescence image of the sensor weakened slightly with the addition of 2 μg/L arsenic and gradually quenched to dark blue when the arsenic was increased to 20 μg/L. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing concentration of AsH3 and the F0-F values gradually increased, which showed a good linear relationship in the range of 1–20 μg/L and the fitting equation was y = 5.782x + 4.872 (R2 = 0.991). Arsenic was detected down to 0.36 μg/L based on the 3σ/slope calculation, where σ is the standard deviation of 11 background variations. In conclusion, the fluorescence images of the CDs in Fig. 3C demonstrate noticeable fluorescence quenching that is visible to the naked eye even at a low concentration of 1 μg/L, which corresponds to the concentration of arsenic. The analytical performance of this method is comparable to or better than that of existing fluorometric methods. Its sensitivity is similar to that of larger spectroscopic instruments, but at a lower cost and with greater ease of use (Table S2). Furthermore, the obtained limit of detection was available to reach the threshold set by the WHO International Standards (10 μg/L) and could be detected by this designed method and device. This strategy can achieve visual sensing and shows great practicality for field detection, which cannot be realized by laboratory fluorescence instruments. The reproducibility of the method expressed as relative standard deviation, was approximately 2.90% (n = 11) at an arsenic concentration of 10 μg/L (Fig. 3D). The stability of the prepared paper was also investigated and the results are shown in Fig. S7. The fluorescence images of the prepared paper, taken as RGB values, showed very little variation with storage time and temperature. This indicates that this proposed fluorescence sensor has a good preservation performance, which is favorable for on-site application for arsenic analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3D printed portable device integrated reaction and detection
ce:section.ce:para: To enable this approach to be applied in a field environment and realize a simple and rapid fluorescence sensing of arsenic, an integrated portable device integrated with sample pretreatment and detection was further fabricated using 3D printing technology into a cartridge (18.60 cm * 10.40 cm * 12.40 cm). The physical drawing of the prepared device in all directions is shown in Fig. 4A, and the specific dimensions of the corresponding components are shown in Fig. S8–S11. A digital temperature-controlled heater was homemade to heat the sample placed on the top of the box, where a motor and permanent magnet with a DC motor controller were attached to the bottom of the box. Stirring and heating can promote the generation and volatilization of AsH3. A 12 V rechargeable battery was used to power the heating plate and the magnet motor (Fig. 4B). To demonstrate the excellent heating performance and high heating efficiency of the heating plate in the designed device, the temperature performance of the developed heater was further investigated and showed that it took only 30 s to reach 30ºC by regulating the voltage and then maintained its stability within 15 min (Fig. S12A. After programmed temperature tests, it was found that the unit could also control the temperature in the range of 0–120ºC and reach 120ºC within 7 min. (Fig. S12B). The detection section consists of two UV excitation sources, a customized groove adapted to the paper loading, and a smartphone capture (detailed steps for photographing and data extraction are in section 1.2 of the Supporting Information). The emission sources were designed at a specific angle to the paper groove to enable irradiation efficiency and minimize excitation interference on the image output. The smartphone was mounted using a U-shaped slot on the top of the box, aligned with the paper groove, and over a long pass filter. The whole unit was then controlled by a set of three switches, corresponding to the UV lamps, the heater, and the main power supply for the whole unit (Fig. 4B). The sample heating and sensing could be achieved in this miniaturized device assisted by smartphone capture and output. It is noted that the total cost of the designed device is approximately $56.

The accuracy of the developed SPFFE-based sensor was validated by the determination of a certified reference water sample (GBW08605, 500.00 ± 8.00 μg/L) and the results are summarised in Table 1. The obtained arsenic concentration was in good agreement with that obtained by ICP-MS, the results being in good agreement with the certified values at the 95% confidence level by t-test, demonstrating the accuracy of the proposed method for the determination of arsenic in water samples. To further validate the practicality of the developed strategy, this device was further evaluated to monitor total arsenic in real water samples, the procedure and detailed locations of which are shown in Fig. 5. From Table 1, it is clear that arsenic was successfully detected in the range of 0.87–22.44 μg/L in ten mobile streams and six well water samples, whose results were consistent with ICP-MS and within the acceptable limit. This indicates that the developed method, combined with a portable fluorescence capture device, shows satisfactory arsenic detection performance in real samples. It's noted that all analytical processes, including sample pretreatment and eventual concentration readout, can be performed in a battery-powered portable device, which is an ideal on-site method to achieve field analysis of arsenic without cumbersome instrumentation. This method enables rapid and low-cost detection of arsenic in groundwater in a field setting, as well as the collection of more reliable data by screening groundwater for exceedances. This data can be used in combination with data modeling to evaluate the risk of arsenic exposure in water and to create risk maps.

In this work, a novel paper-based fluorometric assay for the in situ analysis of arsenic has been developed based on the SPFFE of AsH3-induced generation of AgNPs towards CDs. Due to the efficient reduction of Ag+ by the enriched AsH3 and the high molar extinction coefficient of AgNPs, the detection limit of arsenic in the SPFFE-based assay was as low as 0.36 μg/L. Importantly, the homemade portable integrated device detects the fluorescence image, resulting in quantitative monitoring of arsenic (10 min/per sample). Compared with other devices, this designed device eliminates the need for large instruments and sample collection processes, which greatly simplifies the analysis process and improves accuracy. We believe that this proposed sensor, combined with a low-cost integrated device, could be an important tool for facilitating immediate monitoring of arsenic levels in drinking water sources, identifying safe well areas, and quickly obtaining a global output map revealing the potential hazard from arsenic contamination in groundwater. Currently, the method can only provide offline test results, and it has the potential to facilitate cloud data transfer and GPS sharing for on-site inspections, enabling real-time data reconciliation. In addition, red fluorescence is more easily detected by smartphones than blue fluorescence, which will further increase sensitivity and potentially reduce the time required for this analytical process, making it truly responsive to the need for timely detection in the field. The method is expected to have broader applications beyond drinking water testing.

Simin Ye: Methodology. Wenhui Yang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Liqing Ye: Methodology, Data curation. Yuke Wu: Data curation. Xi Wang: Investigation. Hong Luo: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Jinyi Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chengbin Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yurong Deng: Methodology. Ke Huang: Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22376144, 22104101, and 22076128) and Science & Technology Department of Sichuan Province (No. 24NSFSC6600) for their financial support. We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for microscopy work and we would be grateful to Hui Wang for her help with SEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.
Supporting Information is available and includes materials, instrumentation, partial experimental steps, Fig. S1 to S12, and Table S1 to S2.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2024.134038.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.
